segments.text: [MUSIC PLAYING]

AJA HAMMERLY: Welcome, everyone
here at Shoreline and everyone

tuning in on the live stream,
to "What's New in Firebase."

We're here to get
everyone up to speed

on all of the great
new things in Firebase

that help you build,
test, deploy, run,

and monitor your
applications easily.

I'm Aja, Director of Developer
Relations in Developer X. And--

SEBA GNAGNARELLA: And
I'm Seba, Director

of Engineering for Developer
X Frameworks and Services.

So before we go
too far in, let's

make sure everyone
knows what Firebase is.
Who here has heard of Firebase?
Come on, raise your hand.
Don't be shy.
Come on.
Who here has used Firebase?
Good.
Good.
What about Firebase Studio?
Let's make some noise.
Come on.
[AUDIENCE CHEERS]

What about other
Firebase services?
Sweet.

So Firebase is Google's
platform to prototype, build,

deploy, and run
modern AI powered
experiences your users love.
What makes Firebase so special?
Well, you know very well.

First, it's a
comprehensive platform.

Second, it's very
easy to get started.

We gave you a well-lit path for
you to accomplish your goals.

And then there is no
need to hack together

a solution from two blog
posts and three online forums.
But we don't stop there.

Firebase is backed by GCP's
infrastructure, security,

and reliability, and
it's used by millions
of businesses around the world.

Most importantly, it's
extensible and flexible.

That means that you can decide
which parts of the platform

makes the most sense
for you, and then

bring the tools and
services you need.

As some of you know, we have an
extensive portfolio of products.

Today, we're going
to focus on what's

new since the last time we
met at Next six weeks ago.

Aja and I organized the
content around the steps

you follow to launch
your applications--

prototype and build,
test, deploy it, and run.

AJA HAMMERLY: So we're going
to start with the prototype

and build stage because, well,
that's where most applications
and projects start.

And the big news in the
prototype and build stage--
next slide--

is Firebase Studio, which,
you know, awesome product.
There we go.
We launched that at Next.

And in the weeks since then, we
have had 1.5 million workspaces
created.

That is a lot of
amazing applications.

Seba, what's the coolest
thing you've seen?

SEBA GNAGNARELLA: Oh, I've
seen hundreds of applications

from developers of many kinds,
from an expense management

tool to a car racing
game, from a storytelling

app to an app that allows you
to learn how to speak Japanese.
AJA HAMMERLY: I love that one.

I loved all the
really cool language

learning apps that people came
up with, the Kanji learning one,

one to practice your
conversational language.
It's been amazing.

SEBA GNAGNARELLA: You know
what my favorite one is?
AJA HAMMERLY: What?

SEBA GNAGNARELLA: And I'm
going to geek out a little bit.

There is one that we built
that uses the power of Gemini

to let kids learn
quantum physics.
AJA HAMMERLY: Ooh.

SEBA GNAGNARELLA: That's
pretty impressive.
AJA HAMMERLY: Neat.
So.

SEBA GNAGNARELLA: So for those
of you who haven't heard of it,

Firebase Studio is our
Cloud-based AI workspace.

With Firebase Studio, you can
use AI to create and modify

fully functional apps just using
prompts or jump into Code Mode,

thanks to the power of
a fully extensible IDE

based on Code/OS and
an underlying VM.

In addition to
AI-driven app creation,

we have a set of agents that
help you through the SDLC tasks,

including testing and
documentation, which
are in Private Preview.

AJA HAMMERLY: So I can
use Firebase Studio

to build Go services and
Python backends too, right?
SEBA GNAGNARELLA: You got it.

There are more than 60
templates for common languages

and frameworks, including
Python, Kotlin, Angular,
Flutter, and more.

And we are always working
to add in more of them.

And if you can find
a template that

works for you, Firebase
Studio is highly customizable,

allowing you to
create a development

environment with the
packages and tools you need.

AJA HAMMERLY: So in
the last six weeks,

we've added many requests
from the community
to Firebase Studio.

First of all, in early May,
we incorporated Gemini 2.5,

which enables Firebase
Studio to create

more complex apps with
much more sophisticated UI.

Second, as you heard
just a couple hours ago,

we've added backend integrations
with Firestore and Firebase
Auth.
[AUDIENCE CHEERS]

So when you're prototyping
your applications,

it will understand if this
application needs a database

or auth, set up any
required local environments

so that you can test
your application locally,

and then, when it
comes time to deploy,

it will automatically
provision and configure
those services for you.

We started rolling out
that feature this week,

and it will be rolling out
to the rest of the community
over the next several weeks.

SEBA GNAGNARELLA: So your
application look and feel
is extremely important.

We hear from users
that you wanted

to bring Figma designs
into Firebase Studio
to kickstart your building.

So we partnered with
Builder.io to let you do so.

We also added
significant improvements

for working with app designs
and layouts that include images.

For example, we added features
around placeholder images

and the ability to
use Gemini in runtime
to generate image content.

AJA HAMMERLY: But
words are easy,

so we're going to actually
prove it and do a demo live.

Cross your fingers
for me, folks.
[AUDIENCE CHEERS]
So let's switch to the demo.

Here I am, in Firebase
Studio home screen.

And like Seba said, there's
all these templates here--

Go, Python, and many,
many, many more--
that you can start from.

And that's just a small
selection of them.

But I'm going to
prototype with AI today.
And I want to build a game.
I want to build a game in Go.

Wait, we have a programming
language called that.

I should probably be
slightly more specific, huh?
Go Game.
Let's see how that works.

So the first step of
prototyping with AI

is that Firebase Studio
gives you a blueprint.

You can think of this as
a specification, a PRD,

a technical design document,
and more, all rolled into one.

And it lays out a name for
the app and also the features
it believes that you want.

And this is a place where you
can iterate back and forth

with the AI before
any code is written,

to make sure that the app
is one you want to build.

For this, we've got
a board display.

You got to put your Go
stones on the board,
some stuff around capturing.

Move validation
so I can't cheat.

And then AI hint
generation that'll

help me learn how to play Go
better because I'm really,
really, really bad at this game.

It also lays out the
stack we're going to use,

including Genkit and Gemini,
TypeScript, Next.js, Tailwind
CSS.

And it picked these
lovely, very calm neutrals

that evoke a Go board for
the colors for the app.

So normally, I'd hit
Prototype This App right now,

but we have a ton of content
we need to get through.

So I'm actually going to
switch to one I did earlier.

But to prove that I am not
doing any funny business here,
I'm going to scroll up.

And you can actually see
my entire prompt history
in Firebase Studio.

That's one of the
features we have.

So you can look at
your prompts, and you

can see this is the prompt that
I created this app with just
a couple hours ago.
So exact same app.

There's a slight
bit of difference

because there's a
little bit of difference

because we're using
generative AI.
So let's try it out.

Let's open it in a new window,
and let's put some stones on.
That seems to work pretty good.

Let's see what
the AI has to say.

It thinks I should move at
A3 and gives me a whole bunch
of reasons about it.
Oh, pretty good.

Considering it was a two-word
prompt, I'm pretty happy.

But that's not the cool part
of Firebase Studio for me.

The magic trick is
fun, but the cool part

for Firebase Studio for
me is that at any point,

I can switch over
to Code Mode, which

means if I want to
do a code review,

if I want to manually
add some features,

if I want to integrate
with a library that's

custom to my company,
I can do it all here.

I have right here
our Genkit flows

for suggesting that move,
giving that tips it wants.

And I can actually look at this
code and try to understand it.

Or when it comes up, I can
have Gemini explain this to me.

And all of this is available
to me at any moment.

You can see all of
the code is here.

You can see all of the different
code that goes into the app.

And it will also give
me the web preview,

which is just taking a
moment to load right now.
Here's my game.
So I can see it here.
And there's my Gemini window.

So I have full access to
Gemini Interactive Chat

for adding features,
getting access

to reference documentation
just in time,

asking questions about APIs,
adding features to my product.
All of it's available.

Now, it's not perfect, but
I'm pretty happy with it.
So let's publish.

And I'm going to actually
do this live on stage.

So we're going to
create a rollout.

This is going to push
to Firebase Studio,

and it's going to
do a proper rollout.

We're going to do a
build of static assets.

We're going to push those
static assets to CDN.

This isn't just a
little bit of a rollout.

This is a real,
production-grade rollout.

So this actually takes
a couple of minutes.

And as I said, plenty of
content to get through today.

So we're going to
come back to this

at the end of the
presentation when

we get to the deploy
stage of our SDLC loop.

And we're going to
see how it works,

and we're going to show you
some of the features Firebase

Studio offers you
after you've deployed.
[APPLAUSE]
Back to the slides.

SEBA GNAGNARELLA: Let's
go back to the slides.
Aja, that's super cool.

I might finally
learn how to play Go.

I'm really, really
excited to use your app.

So we recognize that
bringing AI into your apps
can be challenging.

A year ago, we launched
Vertex in Firebase,

making it a lot easier,
and it's already

used by thousands of
customers in production.

Today, we are evolving Vertex in
Firebase into Firebase AI Logic.
[APPLAUSE]
Thank you.

Firebase AI Logic is
one of the easiest ways

to integrate,
innovate, and deploy
AI seamlessly in your apps.

In addition to the
Vertex API, you

can now use Firebase
AI Logic Client

SDKs to connect with
the Gemini Developer
API, which has a free tier.

Also, we are launching
experimental support

for hybrid inference
on Chrome browsers.

You can decide whether you want
to run inference locally using
Gemini Nano or in the Cloud.

And last but not
least, we are launching

Unity support, including
support for Android XR.

We are also expanding
AI Monitoring,

which was also previously
only available for Genkit,

to let you monitor
AI Logic Client SDKs.

In a single pane
of glass, you can

see things such as
latency, error rates,
tokens used, and even traces.
But we didn't stop there.
Aja, can you tell us more?
AJA HAMMERLY: Of course I can.

So one of the other pieces of
Firebase AI Logic is Genkit.

Genkit, if you're not familiar,
is our open-source toolkit

for building
powerful AI backends.

It offers libraries, tools,
and out-of-the-box integrations

for many popular models
and Cloud Services.

And the community has given us
a ton of feedback on Genkit.

And I'm happy to say
that we've heard you,

and we're thrilled
to deliver the things
that you've been asking for.

At Next, we officially launched
Python support in Genkit,

and we've been working hard
at refining that and also

improving our Go support
over the last several weeks.
But that's not all.

We've introduced Dynamic
Model Registration,

which means you no longer need
to wait for a Genkit update
to use the latest models.

And today, we're excited
to announce Genkit.dev.

This is your new go-to site
for the most up-to-date

documentation, code examples
that you need to effectively use
Genkit in your applications.
So awesome.
Such a happy audience.
So that's AI in the backend.

But backends have other
needs too, like storing data.

What do we have for people
who need to store data, Seba?

SEBA GNAGNARELLA: Well, almost
every app needs a database.

And you've been asking
for a Firebase relational
database for a while.
We hear you loud and clear.

And a year ago,
right here at I/O,

we announced Firebase
Data Connect.
[CHEERS, APPLAUSE]

Firebase Data Connect
connects your application

to data in Cloud
SQL for Postgres
through secure serverless APIs.

We built Data Connect
on one premise--

you write the query,
we do the rest.
But what does it mean?

For every query you write,
we create an API endpoint,

we define an auth policy
for that endpoint,

we generate strongly typed SDKs
in languages such as Kotlin,

Dart, Swift, and Node.js, and we
create tool definitions in case

you want to use your queries
as tools with an LLM.

This significantly
accelerates development
and reduces time to value.

At Next, we announced
Firebase Data Connect GA.

And today, we are releasing
two new exciting features.

The first one is Advanced
Query Improvements.

Now you can perform more
sophisticated data retrievals

with transactions that support
additional server values
and multi-step capabilities.

And the second one is Schema
and Query Generation agent.

When you are looking to
incorporate Firebase Data

Connect into your app, Gemini
will turn your app idea

into schemas, queries,
and mutations.

This agent is available in the
Firebase Console and Firebase

Studio, as well as in the VS
Code Extensions for Gemini Code
Assist and Data Connect.
Let's check it out really quick.
Let's jump to this laptop.
There you go.

So here I'm in the
Firebase Console.

Let's say I'm building an
application for TV show reviews,
right?
And I want to add Data Connect.

So instead of having to
think, OK, I'm building a TV

shows review app, what
are the entities I need,

how they relate to each other,
and most importantly, how

I configure that in Data
Connect using GraphQL,

I can use the power of Gemini
and the power of our Data

Connect agent just to prompt
what I'm trying to build.

So I'm going to tell our Data
Connect agent that "App that

helps track user
reviews for TV shows."
Let's see what happens.
So this is Gemini.

Right now, the agent
is analyzing my prompt,

it's figuring out, OK, what
are the entities that I

need to do exactly
what I've been asked,
and configuring the schema.
And here it is.

I have the visual
version on the left,

and I have the GraphQL
version here on the right.
So let's see what we have.
We have a user.
We have a TV show.
The TV show has episodes.
That's pretty smart.

User can watch TV shows, and
then user can review episodes
that they have watched.

That's pretty,
pretty impressive.

So let's say I want
to do something else.

Let's say I want to track
the cast for each episode,

such as actors, producers,
and the directors.
Let's see what we have.

The agent is thinking
through the process,

understanding my prompt, using
the previous schema context,

using the context of
the full application.
Boom.
[CHEERS, APPLAUSE]
It made the changes.

Let's see what new
attributes it added.

So now I have a crew member
that has a name and a role
and a bio and a photo URL.
Gemini is pretty smart.

And then it has-- so that crew
member is related to an episode.

And it added a feature so
users can follow each other.

So the next step is I'm
going to deploy this schema.

I'm going to-- this
will automatically
generate my Cloud SQL instance.
It's provisioning.
It's provisioning the schema.

It's provisioning
the data sources.
So this might take a second.
And here we are.

Now we have a live database
with the schema for our TV shows
review app.
What is the next step?

I need to add operations, such
as retrieving data, modifying
data.

I don't need to do
that manually anymore.

If I go to operations, I can
also use Gemini and our agent
to generate those operations.

So I click a button and
Gemini will automatically

understand the context
of the application,

understand the schema,
and suggest some of them.

For example, add the
TV show to the list,

get the TV show, create
a list, update the list.

So let's say I want
to create a new one.
So I create that.
I'm using natural language.

I can define the
operation I want.

So for example, "Give me all the
episodes for a cast member using

part of the name and
role as parameters."
Voila!

Now I have a method that
takes part of the name,

it takes the role, and the agent
created the GraphQL definition
for that query.
So let's edit.
I'm getting ambitious here.
Let's edit.
Let's do one last edit.
"Add the TV show data."

Because right now, I only have
the data for the episodes.
And here it is.

It added the data
for the TV shows.
Pretty impressive, right?
[CHEERS, APPLAUSE]
All right.
That's all I have for now.
Let's go back to slides.

AJA HAMMERLY: So another thing
that developers have told us

that they struggle
with is verifying

phone numbers for users
of their applications.

And today, we're talking
about Firebase Phone Number

Verification, which
is an exciting update

for any developer currently
using SMS and one-time codes

in order to validate
phone numbers.

Firebase Phone Number
Verification moves end users

from an SMS-based workflow
that is somewhat error

prone, because they have to
type in their phone number,

and then they have
to type in the code

you gave them, to a method
where, with their permission,

you can verify the phone number
directly with the carrier,

so way less copying and
pasting, way fewer steps,
way less chances of errors.

Firebase Phone
Number Verification

is coming this summer,
starting with Android,

and both iOS and web support
are planned after Android.

If you want to know
more, or you want

to give us feedback on what
regions we should start with,

you can scan the QR code to
find out more about the program
and give us that feedback.

So got a bunch of
devs in the audience.

What comes after
prototype and built?

Everyone's favorite
part of the SDLC--
testing.
SEBA GNAGNARELLA: Yeah!
AJA HAMMERLY: Testing.
SEBA GNAGNARELLA: Woo!

AJA HAMMERLY: And
of course, Firebase

has several things new to
talk about in this space.

First of all, one of the things
that as we learn more about AI

and we build more
AI apps, we know

that applications that use AI
need different kinds of testing.

AI Testing agent, which is part
of Firebase Studio, and Gemini

Code Assist, systematically
test your applications

by simulating
adversarial attacks,

and it also measures and
benchmarks your LLM performance
while it's doing it.

SEBA GNAGNARELLA:
So at Firebase,

we are not only
thinking about how

you test the AI models you
use, but we are completely

redefining how you test
your full application.

Available today in
Private Preview,

the App Testing
agent uses Gemini

to create tests for
your mobile applications

using natural
language that then you

can run in a multitude of
physical and virtual devices.

All you need to do is specify
the goal for your test,

and the agent will figure out
where to tap, how to scroll,

and what text to enter
to accomplish that goal.
Let's take a quick look at it.
All right.

So I built this
application, which

is a way of reinventing the
way users search for travel.

Instead of doing the location
search and dates and whatnot,

they use a prompt to tell us
where they want to travel.

I want to test this application,
but most importantly, I

want to automate the testing,
and I want to make sure
that the tests are reliable.

And every time I change
my UI, I want to make sure
that my tests don't break.

So for that, I'm going to
use our App Testing agent.

So I'm here in the Firebase
Console in our product
called Lab Distribution.

And I have this
tab for test cases.

So the first thing
I'm going to do

is I'm going to
write a test case.

So I'm going to call it,
"Find Me a Trip to Spain."

And I'm going to
use natural language

search to tell the agent what
I expect the agent to do.

So the first step is,
"Pick the AI option."

The second is, "Enter a dreamy
prompt about the trip to Spain."

The tool lets me provide
hints to the agent.

So in case that it
doesn't know what to do,
we help them move forward.

So I'm going to tell the agent,
"Hide the keyboard and click
Submit."

And as the last step is,
"From the list of results,

pick the second one, unless
there is only one available."

And the success criteria is,
let's say, knowing my app,

is "The first day of
the itinerary shows."

So as simple as that, I
can define a test case
for my application.
I'm going to save it.
And the next step is to run it.

Our App Testing
agent lets me pick

from a multitude of virtual
and physical devices.

In this case, I'm testing
against a physical Pixel 9

Pro that is in our
data centers, but you

can pick from a multitude
of other devices available.
So I'm going to scroll down.

I'm going to leave
this test running,
but this can take a minute.

So let's look at one of
the previous results I got.
I go to the details.

And I can see, step-by-step,
how the agent navigated
in the scripts.

So for example, where
it tapped, what data it

used, how it used my hints,
how it created the dreamy tip

definition, and
last but not least,

how it used that
definition to pull results.

Look at all the steps
it went through.

And I didn't tell the
agent how to do this.

It is something that the
agent figured out by its own.
And the last step--

I'm going to jump
to the last step--
goal completed.
Day one of the itinerary.
Pretty, pretty impressive.
[CHEERS, APPLAUSE]

All right, let's go
back to the slides.

Aja, we are in the
home stretch here.

We built and prototype
a beautiful application.
We tested it.

Now, how do we make it
available to the world

so it turns into
the next unicorn?

AJA HAMMERLY: Well, Firebase
has stuff for that, of course.

Let's talk about
Firebase App Hosting.

Firebase App Hosting is our
opinionated, serverless web

hosting solution for
modern full-stack web
frameworks like Next.js.

These full-stack
web frameworks make

it easier for developers to
build really cool applications

and get the benefit of things
like server-side rendering that

improves SEO, reduces
initial load times,

and many other
things that we want.

And you get to do it
all in JavaScript.
But-- there's always a but--

to deploy one of
these is way more

complicated than what
I do for my blog, which

is shove a bunch of static
files at a CDN and call it good.

To do this, you actually
need a build step

to build those static files,
you need a server running

for your server-side
rendering, and you still

need that CDN to serve all
the files you create out

to your audience
and your community.

You kind of need it
all, and you need it all
hooked together in a pipeline.

And that is what Firebase
App Hosting does for you.
It does all of that.

We launched Firebase App
Hosting to GA at Next,
but we did not let off the gas.

Firebase App Hosting has
a bunch of new features,

including deploying from
the local Firebase CLI

so you don't have
to configure GitHub

or pushing to a source control
to trigger a new build.

We also added Terraform support,
something that a lot of folks
were asking for.

So you can now build and
deploy your own container image
using Terraform.

And we have automatic
configuration of the Firebase
SDKs and the Emulator Suite.
That was a bunch of words.
Let's talk about what it means.

This means that if you're
running your app locally using

the App Hosting
emulator, you also

can have the emulators running
for other Firebase services you

use, like Auth and Firestore,
and those emulated services

will be used automatically
instead of production

because we don't develop
against production, right?
Right.
OK.

And of course, Firebase
App Hosting seamlessly

integrates with Firebase Studio,
which is what you saw me deploy
to earlier.
So cross fingers, drum roll.

Can we switch back over to
my machine for another demo?
[SCATTERED APPLAUSE]
It rolled out successfully.
And I can show you this.

We've got our live app running
on Firebase App Hosting.
And still get my AI suggestion.
SEBA GNAGNARELLA: Woo!
[CHEERS, APPLAUSE]
AJA HAMMERLY: It's a deployment.
But this is the part I love.

It gives me information about
how many requests I've had.

And yes, I did actually
try a couple on stage

while Seba was demoing to
make sure we had some traffic.

And it also gives you
really important information

that I want as a
developer who's running
an app that people are using.

It gives me information on
the errors that I'm seeing,

the classes of errors,
the number of requests,

and I can get details on all of
this in the Firebase Console.

And that is what I really
love about Firebase Studio.
We started with an idea.
We prototyped with AI.
We went to Code Mode.

We talked about the
ways we can test,

including things like
AI Testing agent,

which are available in Firebase
Studio, and App Testing agent.
We deployed.

And now I'm running
in production,

and I have access to
all the information

I need to monitor and
keep my apps successful.

And that's what I love
about Firebase Studio.

It really has everything,
start to finish,

and then back again to
start for a developer.
It supports the entire SDLC.
And it worked.
I love when the demo--
I love when the demo works.
[CHEERS, APPLAUSE]

So let's go back to the slides
because we have a couple minutes

left and a couple more
things to show y'all.
SEBA GNAGNARELLA: All right.
Let's go back to the slides.

And speaking of
seamlessly integrations,

today we are announcing
Firebase MCP support.
[CHEERS, APPLAUSE]

The Mobile Context
Protocol, MCP,

is an open standard
designed to enhance

the capabilities of AI agents
and large language models.

It acts like a universal
remote control or a USB port

for AI applications,
standardizing

how they connect to and interact
with external tools, data
sources, and services.

Now, you can use the Firebase
MCP server from the CLI

and use it to access the
power of Firebase services

from your favorite agents, and
soon from Firebase Studio too.
That's all for now.

If you want to learn
more about the things

we presented here today, do
not miss our Video On Demand
sessions.

Or if you are here in
person, please join us
in our Cloud Q&A space.

And as always, don't
forget to visit our blog.
